chunk_summary: This section discusses feeding strategies for young infants with chronic cardiorespiratory disease, emphasizing time-limited oral feeds, caloric density targets, and early tube supplementation to optimize growth and reduce feeding fatigue.
learning_objectives: Understand the challenges of feeding infants with CHD or preterm CLD, including fluid restriction and feeding fatigue.
Learn to set time limits for oral feeds and identify triggers for tube supplementation.
Apply caloric density adjustments to meet energy needs within fluid restrictions.
Recognize clinical signs, such as prolonged feeding times and poor weight gain, that necessitate intervention.
key_facts: Infants with CHD or preterm CLD often require 120–150 kcal/kg/day while maintaining fluid intake around 100 mL/kg/day.
Oral feeding sessions should be capped at 20–30 minutes to prevent excessive energy expenditure.
Prolonged feeding times and growth failure are markers for escalating to NG tube supplementation.
In acute severe heart failure, NG feeding can reduce feeding work and improve caloric delivery.
Weight velocity should be reassessed within 7–10 days after implementing feeding adjustments.
reference_quote: Prolonged feed time with growth failure should trigger escalation rather than repeated reassurance. (Source: Indian Academy of Pediatrics, Standard Treatment Guidelines: Heart Failure in Children)
document_key_topics: Feeding strategies for infants with CHD or CLD
Caloric density adjustments and fluid restrictions
Early intervention with NG tube feeding
